title: TPU v1 · SDC as the Synthesis Control Plane: 700 MHz Clock, I/O Delays, and Timing Exceptions
oneLine: Synthesis does not know target speed, external timing, or which paths may take multiple cycles. SDC supplies that intent: create_clock sets an illustrative 1.43 ns/700 MHz target, I/O delays reserve external budget, and false/multicycle paths describe asynchronous or slow behavior. Missing or incorrect constraints make the tool optimize the wrong objective—garbage in, garbage out.

legend.name: create_clock (defines the optimization target)
legend.desc: create_clock defines period and waveform, telling synthesis the cadence for the design. TPU v1 runs at 700 MHz, about 1.43 ns per cycle; the exact script is unpublished. Period drives buffering, sizing, and replication and anchors setup/hold analysis.

legend.name: set_input_delay / set_output_delay (I/O budget)
legend.desc: Inputs do not arrive exactly at the chip clock edge, and downstream logic consumes part of the output cycle. I/O delays describe time used outside the chip so synthesis knows the remaining internal budget. Omitting them makes boundary timing unrealistically optimistic or pessimistic.

legend.name: set_false_path / set_multicycle_path (exceptions)
legend.desc: Not every path must complete in one cycle. Asynchronous paths crossing through synchronizers can be excluded with set_false_path; intentionally slow paths can receive N cycles with set_multicycle_path. Missing exceptions wastes area through overconstraint, while incorrect exceptions hide real violations.

legend.name: Constrained path: launch FF → logic → capture FF
legend.desc: Synthesis sees many register-to-register paths. Every MAC stage in the systolic array fits this model. After subtracting I/O and clock uncertainty from 1.43 ns, the remainder is the delay budget for combinational logic.

legend.name: Wrong constraints = wrong optimization (GIGO)
legend.desc: The tool trusts SDC. A loose period may report success while silicon misses 700 MHz; a missing false path may trigger pointless buffering and area/power growth. Contradictory or incomplete constraints make clean reports meaningless.

steps.cap: ① Establish clocks: create_clock sets an illustrative 700 MHz/1.43 ns core target, requiring every ordinary register-to-register path to complete within the cycle.
② Define boundaries: set_input_delay and set_output_delay account for time consumed by external PCIe- and DDR3-side logic, leaving a realistic on-chip budget.
③ Declare exceptions: mark synchronizer-based PCIe↔core CDC paths false and intentionally slow paths multicycle so the tool does not force one-cycle timing.
④ Reserve margin and model loads: set_clock_uncertainty covers jitter/skew, while set_load and set_driving_cell make timing reflect realistic interfaces.
⑤ With complete constraints, synthesis closes the true 1.43 ns target and QoR is credible; one missing exception or loose period immediately redirects optimization.

before: Define every clock and period: Inventory clock domains such as PCIe, core array, and DDR3. For the 700 MHz core, use an illustrative create_clock -period 1.43. Without clocks, the tool cannot define “fast enough.”
Allocate I/O timing: Encode delay already consumed by upstream and downstream circuitry with set_input_delay and set_output_delay. Unconstrained boundaries force guesses and distort optimization.

Inventory timing exceptions: Collaborate with design and verification to identify true asynchronous synchronizer crossings and intentionally multicycle paths. This list drives set_false_path and set_multicycle_path; omissions create over- or underconstraint.

Set uncertainty and operating conditions: Before a real clock tree exists, reserve jitter/skew and synthesis margin with set_clock_uncertainty, and select the appropriate PVT operating condition and library.
Define constraint acceptance criteria: Require clean parsing, no missing objects, no unconstrained ports or paths, and documented, traceable timing exceptions.
during: Core rule: constraints are the optimization objective. The tool does not infer intent. A 1.43 ns period targets 700 MHz; 2 ns targets only 500 MHz. Every area, power, buffering, and replication tradeoff follows the written SDC.

Establish clocks before exceptions. The dependency is clock → I/O budget → uncertainty → exceptions. False and multicycle paths subtract from an already defined clock relationship; exceptions have no meaning without clocks.

Underconstraint is more dangerous than visible violations. Missing I/O or path constraints can produce clean reports because the tool sees no requirement. Finding unconstrained ports and paths is as important as fixing negative slack.

Overconstraint and careless exceptions are also harmful. Marking a real path false hides a silicon failure; an unrealistically tight period causes excessive buffering and replication. Every exception needs evidence and must never be added merely to clean a report.

Treat CDC paths explicitly. Declare PCIe↔core asynchronous clocks with set_clock_groups or false paths, or constrain synchronizer paths individually. Otherwise unrelated clocks create false setup violations or unsafe optimization.

Label methodology and numbers honestly: SDC commands are standard across Design Compiler and Genus flows. TPU v1 publishes 700 MHz, 28 nm, and the array structure, but not its constraint scripts; 1.43 ns and all snippets here are illustrative conversions.

result.label: Role of constraints
result.value: The synthesis objective itself; wrong constraints redirect optimization (GIGO)

after: Confirm all constraints were read: Inspect logs for missing objects, mismatched port names, or undefined clocks. One mistyped name can silently disable a constraint and invalidate optimization.

Find unconstrained ports and paths: Use report_constraint and check_timing to locate endpoints not covered by clocks. Complete them or declare justified exceptions; otherwise clean timing is false confidence.
Audit timing exceptions: Review every false and multicycle path for written rationale and verify it truly crosses domains or takes multiple cycles. Never exclude a path that requires closure.

Check whether QoR reflects the right problem: Review WNS/TNS, area, and power to separate real RTL/architecture failures from artifacts of unreasonable constraints. Revise SDC and resynthesize iteratively.

Hand SDC to backend and signoff STA: Deliver validated constraints with the gate netlist. Place-and-route and signoff STA reuse the same intent with real clock trees and extracted parasitics, so SDC remains the control plane end to end.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the 256×256 array, 700 MHz, 28 nm, PCIe host interface, and weight-stationary design are published. SDC commands, all snippets, and the 1.43 ns conversion are illustrative standard EDA practice; TPU scripts and QoR are not public.)
